coredata.dc:title: In-situ synthesis of carbon dots-embedded europium metal-organic frameworks for ratiometric fluorescence detection of Hg2+ in aqueous environment
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Hao
Wang, Xiaoqiong
Wu, Ning
Xu, Mengni
Wang, Mingyue
Zhang, Longwen
Yang, Wu
coredata.dc:description: A novel dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor (CDs@Eu-MOFs) has been synthesized successfully by encapsulating water-soluble fluorescent CDs into the chambers of Eu-MOFs via a simple one-pot hydrothermal method, which can be used for the detection of Hg2+ in environmental water samples. The synthesized CDs@Eu-MOFs inherited simultaneously the excellent luminescence performance of Eu3+ and CDs and exhibited good structural and fluorescence stability in aqueous solution. The presence of Hg2+ changed the fluorescence intensity of CDs, while the fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOFs hardly changed, which could be used as the recognition part and the reference part respectively to construct the ratiometric fluorescence sensor. The obtained Hg2+ fluorescent probe showed a wide linear range (0–300 μM) and a low detection limit (0.12 nM). The developed method can be used as a multi-functional fluorescent sensor with high sensitivity and good selectivity for the determination of Hg2+ in actual water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Eu-MOFs
Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Fluorescence sensing
Mercury ion
Green synthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent determination of actual water sample
Detection of Hg2+ in the actual water sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a, b) images of CDs; SEM(c), TEM(d-f) images of CDs@Eu-MOFs.

(a) XRD patterns of the simulation Eu-MOFs (black line), the synthesized Eu-MOFs (red line) and CDs@Eu-MOFs(blue line); (b) UV–vis absorption spectra (black line), excitation (blue line) and emission spectra (red line) of CDs@Eu-MOFs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The excitation (black line) and emission (red line) spectra of solid-state CDs@Eu-MOFs (the inset is the corresponding luminescence picture under UV-light irradiation of 365 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The fluorescence emission spectra (a) and the corresponding ratio fluorescence intensity (F430/F614) (b) of the CDs@Eu-MOFs in presence of various metal ions.

(a) The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@Eu-MOFs in the presence of different Hg2+ concentrations; (b) the linear relationship of the ratio fluorescence intensity (F430/F614) and concentration of Hg2+.
Schematic illustration of the synthetic route of the CDs, CDs@Eu-MOFs and detection mechanism for Hg2+.

head.ce:title: In-situ synthesis of carbon dots-embedded europium metal-organic frameworks for ratiometric fluorescence detection of Hg2+ in aqueous environment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE, Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, Gansu International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Water-Retention Chemical Functional Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE
Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province
Gansu International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Water-Retention Chemical Functional Materials
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE, Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, Gansu International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Water-Retention Chemical Functional Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor (CDs@Eu-MOFs) has been synthesized successfully by encapsulating water-soluble fluorescent CDs into the chambers of Eu-MOFs via a simple one-pot hydrothermal method, which can be used for the detection of Hg2+ in environmental water samples. The synthesized CDs@Eu-MOFs inherited simultaneously the excellent luminescence performance of Eu3+ and CDs and exhibited good structural and fluorescence stability in aqueous solution. The presence of Hg2+ changed the fluorescence intensity of CDs, while the fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOFs hardly changed, which could be used as the recognition part and the reference part respectively to construct the ratiometric fluorescence sensor. The obtained Hg2+ fluorescent probe showed a wide linear range (0–300 μM) and a low detection limit (0.12 nM). The developed method can be used as a multi-functional fluorescent sensor with high sensitivity and good selectivity for the determination of Hg2+ in actual water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Hg2+ is considered to be a highly toxic heavy metal ion, distributed in various aspects of the environment and biota [1–3]. It is a widespread biological accumulative pollutant that can accumulate through the food chain or high doses of exposure and pose a serious threat to the environment and human health [4–6]. Even a minor quantity of bioaccumulated mercury can cause a variety of diseases like neurological disorders, digestive, kidney, and so on [7–9]. The United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) sets the maximum allowable level of Hg2+ in drinking water is only 10 nM (2 ppb) [10]. Therefore, from the perspective of environmental protection and health, great efforts have been made globally to address the environmental problem of mercury pollution, based on the development of new materials and technologies that can selectively detect Hg2+ [11,12]. Among all kinds of established Hg2+ detection technologies, fluorimetry is favored by more and more researchers because of its unique superiorities such as high sensitivity, simple operation and fast response time [13,14]. So far, many fluorescence systems for the detection of Hg2+ have been developed, such as DNA enzymes [15], polymers [16], single-walled carbon nanotubes [17], metal nanoclusters [18] and semiconductor quantum dots [19]. However, most of the established sensors rely on the intensity change of the luminescence signal generated by a single emission center, which may cause an erroneous response due to changes in the external environment [20]. Consequently, it is vital to establish a ratiometric sensor based on the intensity ratio of two emission centers to break through the limitation of a single-emission fluorescence sensor [21].

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of luminescent nanomaterial with quantum dimensions, have attracted worldwide attention because of their excellent optical properties, chemical stability, low toxicity, easy functionalization, and high biocompatibility [22–25]. Compared with traditional fluorescent materials such as organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, CDs have neither the high toxicity of inorganic semiconductor quantum dots nor the disadvantages of unstable luminescence and easy photo-bleaching of organic dyes [26,27]. In recent years, there has been an upsurge of research on the synthesis of CDs by employing green natural materials as carbon source [28,29]. At present, many studies have reported the preparation of CDs from biological raw materials, such as actinidia [30], spinach [31], milk [32], orange juice [33] and honey [34]. These natural substances contain a large number of carbohydrates, proteins, sugars and other organic matters, which can provide a large number of carbon atoms and heteroatoms for preparing CDs, so they have gradually become the focus and direction of current research [35–37]. As we all know, black wolfberry is rich in protein, polysaccharide, amino acids, vitamins, minerals, trace elements and other nutrients, thus it is a good raw material precursor for the preparation of CDs [38].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), a kind of hybrid material composed of metal ions or clusters and organic ligands through coordination bonds, exhibit good load capacity, large specific surface area, controllable aperture, adjustable emission characteristics and predictable nanostructure. Accordingly, they have been thought to be very promising in the field of chemical sensing [39–43]. However, it is often difficult to directly obtain a specific function [44,45]. In recent years, more and more research interests focus on developing functional MOFs by encapsulation strategy to achieve inorganic ions and organic small molecules detection [46,47]. This strategy combines the advantages of MOFs and nanoparticles or guest molecules to obtain superior composites with better performance than individual components, further satisfying the needs of researchers [48,49]. Take zeolite imidazole framework material (ZIF-8) for example, which has been used to encapsulate some functional carbon points (CDs) for various applications [50].

Inspired by these results, we at first prepared blue fluorescent CDs with black wolfberry as a carbon source through a hydrothermal method in this work, and then introduced it into the synthesis environment of Eu-MOFs to obtain successfully a new fluorescence composite material (CDs@Eu-MOFs). The synthetic CDs@Eu-MOFs inherited simultaneously the excellent luminescence performance of Eu3+ and CDs, exhibited good structural stability and fluorescence stability in aqueous solution, and could be used to determine Hg2+ in environmental water samples (Scheme 1 ). The presence of Hg2+ strongly quenched the fluorescence of CDs, but did not influence the Eu-MOFs fluorescence emission. So they could be used as the recognition part and the reference part respectively to construct the ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solution. The obtained Hg2+ fluorescent probe showed a wide linear range (0–300 μM) and a low detection limit (0.12 nM). On the basis of the luminescent behaviors of the CDs@Eu-MOFs, a multi-functional fluorescent sensor was developed for the detection of Hg2+ in actual water samples.

All reagents in this experiment were analytical grade and used directly without further purification. EuCl3·6H2O (366.41 g mol−1), 2-aminoterephthalic acid (atpt) and 1,10-phenanthroline (phen) were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ammonia solution (25%) was purchased from Tianjin Standard Science and Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Aqueous solutions of Zn2+, Ni2+, Na+, Mg2+, K+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+ and Co2+ were prepared from nitrate salts. The dialysis membranes (molecular weight cut off (MWCO: 500)) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Black wolfberry was purchased from a local supermarket.

The optical performances of the synthesized CDs and CDs@Eu-MOFs were analyzed by UV757CRT UV–visible spectrophotometer and FluoroMax-4 spectrophotometer. The morphologies and composition of the samples were measured by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive spectrum (EDS). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were acquired on a FEI TecnaiG2F20S-TWIN transmission electron microscope (USA) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns were recorded on a Bruker X-ray diffractometer (Germany) using CuKα radiation at 40 kV and 40 mA. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was collected within KBr slices using a Digilab FTS-3000 IR spectrometer.

CDs were directly synthesized using the hydrothermal method reported in the literature [38]. Briefly, 1 g of dried black wolfberry was ground into powder and added 30 mL of deionized water. 30 mL of the mixture was filtered through the vacuum to obtain a solution without solid residue. Then, the above solution and 1 mL ammonia (25%) solution were mixed and transferred to a Teflon-lined pressure vessel of 25 mL capacity and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. Finally, the obtained solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous filter head to remove large particles, and the obtained filtrate was further purified using a dialysis membrane (MWCO: 500 Da) for 24 h to obtain the brown-yellow solution of the CDs, which was stored at 4 °C for further use in later experiments.

Pure Eu-MOFs was synthesized according to the reported method in the literature [51], and the synthesis procedure of CDs@Eu-MOFs remained unchanged except adding CDs to the precursor. Typically, EuCl3·6H2O (0.1022 g), atpt (0.077 g), phen (0.056 g), and DI-water (7 mL) were mixed in a 25 mL beaker, 0.84 mL (0.65 M) NaOH aqueous solution was added to the mixture after complete dissolution at room temperature. Then, 200 μL of as-synthesized CDs solution were added, after completely mixing, the mixture was maintained at 160 °C for 12 h. Finally, the desired products were obtained through washing several times with DI water and vacuum drying for 6 h at 60 °C.

The fluorescent determination experiments were conducted on a FluoroMax-4 spectrophotometer at room temperature selecting 365 nm as excitation wavelength (The excitation and emission slit widths: 3 nm). After 10 mg CDs@Eu-MOFs material was dispersed in 100 mL DI-water and oscillated for 5 min to obtain a well-mixed CDs@Eu-MOFs suspension, different concentrations of Hg2+ was added and mixed thoroughly, and then the fluorescence spectra of the mixture solution were scanned at room temperature. Interference ions (Zn2+, Ni2+, Na+, Mg2+, K+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Ag+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+ and Co2+) of the same initial concentration as Hg2+ (10 mL, 10−2 M) were added into the mixture solution to evaluate the selectivity and specificity of the synthetic materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent determination of actual water sample
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical application of the sensor, the sensing behavior of Hg2+ in laboratory tap water samples was further studied. The collected tap water samples were pretreated through an injection filter (0.22 μm) before the experiment, and then added different concentrations of Hg2+. The fluorescence spectra were recorded, F430/F614 value was measured and the content of Hg2+ in the real sample was determined based on the obtained working curve.

The morphology and structure of the synthesized CDs and CDs@Eu-MOFs were observed preliminarily by TEM and SEM. TEM images of CDs indicated that the CDs used in the experiment consisted of amorphous carbon and most of them were 1–3 nm non-clustered spheres ( Fig. 1 a and b). The SEM and TEM images ( Fig. 1c–1e) of CDs@Eu-MOFs showed that the material existed as a tetragonal structure. As shown in Fig. 1f, these CDs were indeed wrapped in Eu-MOFs, indicating the successful formation of CDs@Eu-MOFs composite material. It could be seen from the pore size distribution diagram that the average pore radius of Eu-MOFs was about 15.43 Å (Fig. S3) which further indicated that CDs had been successfully encapsulated into the cavities of Eu-MOFs or adsorbed on the surface on Eu-MOFs.

The XRD patterns of the synthesized Eu-MOFs and CDs@Eu-MOFs coincided with the simulated XRD pattern [51], which fully proved that the synthesis of Eu-MOFs was successful and the addition of CDs had no effect on the main framework structure of Eu-MOFs ( Fig. 2 a). The FT-IR spectra were used to characterize functional groups on the surface of CDs@Eu-MOFs. It could be seen from the FT-IR spectra that the synthesized CDs@Eu-MOFs had similar characteristic absorption bands with Eu-MOFs, which proved that CDs had been doped into Eu-MOFs successfully ( Fig. S4 ). The UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the CDs@Eu-MOFs were showed in Fig. 2b. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs@Eu-MOFs exhibited a strong absorption peak at about 225 nm accompanied by a shoulder around 320 nm, the absorption peak at about 225 nm might be considered as the π-π∗ transition of CC bonds, while the wide absorption peak at 320 nm might be attributed to the n-π∗ transition of CO on the surface of the CDs [54–56]. At the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, CDs solution exhibited a strong emission peak at 430 nm, thus it had a strong blue fluorescence under the ultraviolet lamp (λ = 365 nm). As seen from Fig. 2b, CDs@Eu-MOFs fluorescence probe presented a strong emission peak from CDs at 430 nm, and three weak characteristic emission peaks from Eu3+ at 592 nm, 614 nm and 650 nm, respectively. Furthermore, the CDs@Eu-MOFs suspension was observed to be bright blue under the 365 nm UV light (inset in Fig. 2b), which was consistent with the color of the CDs under the ultraviolet lamp. Apparently, the above analysis results proved that CDs were successfully encapsulated into the cavities of Eu-MOFs.

The solid-state excitation and emission spectra of CDs@Eu-MOFs were assessed at room temperature (Fig. 3 ). As expected, under the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, the synthesized CDs@Eu-MOFs showed 5 characteristic emission peaks of Eu3+ at 580, 592, 614, 650 and 700 nm, which could be attributed to the transitions from 5D0 → 7F0, 5D0 → 7F1, 5D0 → 7F2, 5D0 → 7F3, 5D0 → 7F4, respectively. The fluorescence emission ascribed to CDs at 430 nm was not observed, implying that the agglomerated CDs did not emit. As shown in Fig. S5, when the excitation wavelength increased from 340 nm to 390 nm, the fluorescence intensity of CDs in solution increased gradually and then decreased slowly, and reached its maximum value at 365 nm. At this point, the fluorescence emission band of CDs (430 nm) was consistent with the blue emission band of CDs@Eu-MOFs in solution, indicating that the blue emission band originated from CDs. According to the fluorescence spectra of the CDs@Eu-MOFs with different volumes of CDs, it was clear that the fluorescence intensity was related to the volume of CDs (Fig. S6). The fluorescence intensity of CDs@Eu-MOFs at 430 nm reached the maximum value when the CDs volume was 200 μL, which was the optimal introduction value of CDs in the experimental material.

In addition, the luminescence and structural stability of the material under extreme conditions were further studied to evaluate the feasibility of the material as a fluorescent probe in the water medium and obtain high sensitivity and selectivity. Firstly, the XRD patterns of the materials after water treatment at 373 K for 24 h was almost consistent with the diffraction peak of the untreated sample, implying that CDs@Eu-MOFs possessed excellent structural stability (Fig. S7). Subsequently, the fluorescence responses of CDs@Eu-MOFs probe in different pH values, different storage days and NaCl concentrations were observed (Fig. S8-S10). Obviously, the ratio fluorescence intensities (F430/F614) hardly changed in the wide pH range of 4–10, which indicated that the CDs@Eu-MOFs could be applied to acidic to alkaline environments. Then the effect of storage days on the fluorescence sensor was also explored, it was apparent that the value of F430/F614 almost unchanged after being stored at room temperature for about 7 days. Furthermore, the influence of NaCl concentration was further considered. F430/F614 remained stable when NaCl concentration was 0.2–1.0 mol L−1, indicating that CDs@Eu-MOFs showed good stability in high ionic strength solution. Through the above analysis, it could be concluded that CDs@Eu-MOFs possessed good anti-interference ability and could be better applied to metal ion detection, which further showed it had great potential in practical sensing applications.

Considering that the synthetic CDs@Eu-MOFs possessed a series of excellent characteristics, such as strong blue fluorescence, good structure and fluorescence stability, the fluorescence response of CDs@Eu-MOFs to metal ions in aqueous solution at room temperature was investigated. CDs@Eu-MOFs were dispersed into aqueous solutions of different metal ions (Zn2+, Ni2+, Na+, Mg2+, K+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Ag+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Hg2+ and Co2+) and the fluorescence intensity was recorded in Fig. 4 . It was noteworthy that the fluorescence intensity of CDs@Eu-MOFs at 430 nm was almost completely quenched in the presence of Hg2+, whereas the fluorescence of Eu3+ has hardly changed, which was consistent with the color change under the ultraviolet lamp at 365 nm. According to the above analysis, the ratio fluorescence intensity (F430/F614) of CDs@Eu-MOFs at 430 nm and 614 nm could be utilized to monitor Hg2+. For comparison, the fluorescence responses of individual CDs in different metal ions were evaluated in Fig. S11. Surprisingly, it was found that the fluorescence of CDs was completely quenched by Fe3+, but the fluorescence intensity was only weakened in the presence of Hg2+, instead of being completely quenched. As a result, blue luminescence of CDs in the presence of Hg2+ still remained under UV light irradiation of 365 nm. Eventually, the anti-interference capability of CDs@Eu-MOFs after mixing Hg2+ with various coexisting metal ions was also further determined, which could realize the selective detection of Hg2+ relatively quickly (Fig. S12). Consequently, interference substances had no effect on the detection of Hg2+. Furthermore, the influence of reaction time on the fluorescence intensity of the materials was further discussed. It can be seen from Fig. S13, the fluorescence quenching efficiency reached almost 100% after the addition of Hg2+ for about 3 min, indicating that the material could quickly complete the detection of Hg2+.

Subsequently, the high sensitivity of CDs@Eu-MOFs towards the determination of Hg2+ was further evaluated. As exhibited in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@Eu-MOFs at 430 nm was weakened gradually with the increasing of Hg2+ concentration from 0 to 300 μM, meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity at 614 nm was basically maintained constant, which proved that CDs@Eu-MOFs material was very sensitive to Hg2+. A good linear relationship could be observed between the ratio fluorescence intensity (F430/F614) and the concentration of Hg2+ (Fig. 5b), which could be quantified by a modified Stern-Völmer equation, F430/F614 = Ksv[M] + C, where [M] was the concentration of Hg2+, the Ksv was the apparent quenching constant, and F430 and F614 were the fluorescence intensity of CDs@Eu-MOFs suspension at 430 nm and 614 nm, respectively. Ksv was calculated as −0.0189 M-1 with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9938. Corresponding linear equation was F430/F614 = −1.89 × 10−2 [M] + 9.18. The lowest detection limit (LOD) calculated was about 0.12 nM based on the equation LOD = 3S/K (S/N = 3). The detection limit was not only far lower than the maximum allowable concentration of Hg2+ in drinking water specified by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (10 nM, 2 ppb) [57], but also lower than the maximum level permitted by the European Union (5 nM, 1 ppb) [58]. This method was compared with the previously reported fluorescence sensors used to detect Hg2+ (Table S1). The results showed that the sensitivity of this method was superior to those of some sensors reported in previous literature, which further proved that the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on CDs@Eu-MOFs had a good application prospect in detecting Hg2+ in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in the actual water sample
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of determining Hg2+ in tap water and Yellow River water by CDs@Eu-MOFs was further discussed. As shown in Table S2, the recoveries of Hg2+ in tap water and Yellow River water samples were 99.10–110.6% and 97.53–104.6%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) were 0.89–2.27% and 0.77–1.57%. The results showed that the CDs@Eu-MOFs as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor could be successfully applied to the determination of Hg2+ in practical water samples.

The underlying mechanism of fluorescence quenching caused by Hg2+ was analyzed from the following aspects, respectively. First of all, the influence of structural changes on the fluorescence response was investigated. The XRD pattern of CDs@Eu-MOFs treated with Hg2+ aqueous solution was in perfect agreement with that of the untreated sample, which could exclude the possibility of frameworks collapse (Fig. S14). Subsequently, in order to prove that the MOF structure had the ability to accumulate the analyte and improve the sensing sensitivity, the fluorescence interaction between CDs or CDs@Eu-MOFs and Hg2+ also was further discussed, respectively. As described in Fig. S15, the same concentration of Hg2+ was added into CDs and CDs@Eu-MOFs solutions with the same initial fluorescence intensity, respectively. The fluorescence quenching degree of the MOFs-based nanoprobes was far higher than that of CDs because of the strong adsorption capacity of the Eu-MOFs. This analysis result indicated that Eu-MOFs structure played an essentially vitally role in improving the sensing sensitivity. Eventually, competitive energy absorption was another possible reason for the quenching phenomena. According to the UV–vis absorption spectra, the presence of Hg2+ caused a significant decrease in absorbance of the CDs@Eu-MOFs suspension at 260 nm, and about 15 nm red shift, which could be attributed to the coordination between Hg2+ and some functional groups in CDs [59,60], thus quenching the fluorescence of CDs effectively (Fig. S16). The EDS distribution diagram further illustrated the existence of Hg2+ (Fig. S17). In addition, Eu-MOFs, as the host of CDs and had certain porosity, which could not only avoid the CDs aggregation, but also exhibit a strong adsorption and accumulation capacity for Hg2+, thus improving the specific recognition ability of the materials. The synergistic effect of adsorption and competitive energy absorption greatly improves the sensitivity of the probe.

A novel dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor (CDs@Eu-MOFs) was synthesized successfully, which encapsulated or adsorbed the blue fluorescent water-soluble CDs into the chambers of Eu-MOFs via a simple one-pot method. The as-prepared fluorescent functionalized MOFs not only maintained the excellent optical properties of CDs and Eu3+, but also had good structural and fluorescence stability in aqueous solution. Furthermore, the ratiometric fluorescent probe could overcome the interference of the external environment effectively, such as pH value, storage time and other factors. More importantly, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on CDs@Eu-MOFs could be applied to the detection of Hg2+ in actual water samples. The established ratiometric fluorescence sensor showed a wide linear range (0–300 μM) and a low detection limit (0.12 nM), which was expected to be widely applied in the determination of Hg2+ in environmental water samples. The study provided a general and effective strategy for constructing bi-functional MOFs-based fluorescence sensor for highly sensitive metal ion determination.
The authors declare that there are not competing interests.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: About this paper, no any interest conflict exists.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are very grateful to National Natural Science Foundation of China (21665024) and Northwest Normal University Young Teachers Research Capacity Promotion Plan (NWNU-LKQN-18-23).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2020.10.028.
